tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Within the framework of the seven stages of film production—development, financing, pre-production, production, post-production, marketing, and distribution—identify the film school that offers a dedicated Producing School where students are trained across this entire start-to-finish process in both New York and Los Angeles. Then, describe the structure and key components of its fully online Master of Arts program focused on entrepreneurial producing and innovation. In your response, include: (1) the types of producing degrees, certificates, and workshops offered at the school; (2) how the curriculum prepares students to manage all stages of production; (3) the duration and semester structure of the online MA program; (4) examples of core producing, business, and theory courses in that MA; (5) the kinds of hands-on projects and capstone requirement students complete; and (6) the tuition structure for the online MA program.

Across the widely recognized seven sequential stages of professional film production—from the initial concept phase through public release—identify each stage in order and explain its primary objectives, key personnel or roles involved, and major deliverables or activities that distinguish it from the others. Your answer should clearly differentiate responsibilities such as creative development, financing efforts, on-set coordination, editorial processes, promotional strategy, and release logistics.

An institution that outlines the seven stages of film production—from development and financing through distribution—also offers specialized training for two key creative roles central to those stages: the screenwriter (who develops the script during development and pre-production) and the producer (who guides projects from financing through marketing and distribution). Identify this institution and comprehensively describe: (1) the types of degree programs, conservatory programs, and workshops available through its dedicated screenwriting division, including examples of credentials offered and formats (in-person or online); and (2) the structure, duration, curriculum components, and culminating projects of its fully online master’s degree focused on entrepreneurial producing and innovation. Your answer should clearly distinguish between the offerings for screenwriters and the advanced producing degree, and explain how each supports different stages of the film production process.

In the context of the film production pipeline, the development stage centers on transforming ideas into structured scripts before a project moves into financing and pre-production. Identify all of the fully online, multi-week screenwriting workshops offered by a major U.S.-based film academy that are specifically designed to support this development phase. For each workshop, specify: (1) its full title and duration, (2) its primary creative focus (e.g., feature film, television spec, pilot, comic book, rewrite, or foundational story structure), (3) any stated prerequisites or submission requirements, and (4) the general tuition range and technical/software expectations common across these online workshops. Your response must comprehensively cover every qualifying online workshop and compare their purposes and entry requirements.

It then requires aggregation of information from both the school’s general Producing School offerings and its separate Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation page (Wide), ensuring the respondent must synthesize details about degrees, curriculum, projects, structure, and tuition from multiple sources.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing School Producing School Producing Degrees, Programs, and Workshops A Producer supervises and manages pre-production, filming, and post-production, overseeing al
rounds.answer_a:
The New York Film Academy (NYFA) operates a dedicated Producing School with campuses in both New York and Los Angeles, training students across the entire filmmaking pipeline [1]. NYFA offers a comprehensive range of programs, including Master of Fine Arts (MFA), Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), and Associate of Fine Arts (AFA) degrees in Producing, in addition to 1- and 2-year certificate programs and various short-term workshops [2].

The curriculum is designed to prepare students for all seven stages of production, from development to distribution. This is exemplified in its fully online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation [3]. This 30-credit MA program is completed over two full-time semesters. The curriculum integrates creative producing with modern business strategy through core courses such as **Producing the Feature Film**, **Business of Producing**, **Marketing & Distribution**, and **New Media & Innovation** [3, 4].

Students engage in hands-on work by developing and producing short-form content like web series throughout the program [4]. The capstone requirement is a thesis project where each student creates a comprehensive business and marketing plan for a feature film or television series they have developed, ready for presentation to investors [5]. The tuition structure for the one-year online MA program is $17,468 per semester, totaling $34,936 for the full program [6].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Producing School*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
[2] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Producing Programs*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/programs.php
[3] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/online-programs/ma-entrepreneurial-producing.php
[4] New York Film Academy. (2024). *New York Film Academy Course Catalog 2024*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/forms/catalog-2024-v2.pdf
[5] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation: Curriculum*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/online-programs/ma-entrepreneurial-producing.php#p-curriculum
[6] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Tuition and Fees: Online Programs*. Retrieved from https://www.nyfa.edu/admissions/tuition_fees/online-programs.php
rounds.answer_b:
The **Columbia University School of the Arts** — specifically its **School of Professional Studies** — does not match; rather, the institution is the **Columbia University School of Professional Studies**. However, the film school that offers a dedicated **Producing School** training students across all seven stages in both New York and Los Angeles is the **American Film Institute (AFI) Conservatory** [1]. Yet, the school most precisely matching all criteria — offering a fully online MA in entrepreneurial producing with presence in both cities — is the **New York Film Academy (NYFA)** [2].

NYFA's **Producing School** offers: **(1)** MFA in Filmmaking (Producing focus), **MA in Producing**, BFA programs, AAS degrees, one-year certificates, and short workshops across its NY and LA campuses [2][3].

**(2)** The curriculum trains students to manage **development** (script evaluation, IP acquisition), **financing** (budgeting, investor pitches), **pre-production** (scheduling, casting), **production** (set management), **post-production** (editing oversight), **marketing**, and **distribution** — covering the full production lifecycle [2].

**(3)** The online MA program spans **four semesters over two years**, structured for working professionals with asynchronous and synchronous coursework [3].

**(4)** Core courses include **Producing the Short Film**, **Entertainment Business & Law**, **Budgeting & Scheduling**, **Film History & Theory**, **The Art of the Pitch**, and **Digital Distribution Strategies** [2][3].

**(5)** Students complete hands-on producing projects each semester, including producing short films remotely and collaboratively, culminating in a **capstone thesis project** — a fully produced or packaged feature/series with a business plan and investor deck [3].

**(6)** Tuition for the online MA is structured on a **per-semester basis**, totaling approximately **$30,000–$40,000** for the full program, with financial aid and payment plans available [3].

## References
[1] American Film Institute, "AFI Conservatory – Producing," https://www.afi.com/conservatory/producing/
[2] New York Film Academy, "Producing School," https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/
[3] New York Film Academy, "Online MA in Producing," https://www.nyfa.edu/online/ma-producing/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the New York Film Academy (NYFA) Producing School and recognize its presence in New York and Los Angeles, as well as the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation. So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both agents contain major factual inaccuracies. The ground truth specifies the online MA is one year across three semesters, with tuition at $10,000 per semester plus a technology fee. Agent A incorrectly states it is two semesters and gives an incorrect tuition figure. Agent B incorrectly claims it spans four semesters over two years and provides a vague and inflated tuition estimate. Both therefore fail key checklist items (duration and tuition), which are central to the query. Additionally, both miss or misstate parts of the required degree range and specific structural details.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B has slightly clearer structured formatting, but it also introduces confusion (AFI mention) and includes incorrect program length and tuition. Agent A is more concise but also factually incorrect on major structural elements. Since both contain significant hallucinations on critical checklist items, this results in a Low Quality Tie.

logic_chain: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.question: Across the widely recognized seven sequential stages of professional film production—from the initial concept phase through public release—identify each stage in order and explain its primary objectives, key personnel or roles involved, and major deliverables or activities that distinguish it from the others. Your answer should clearly differentiate responsibilities such as creative development, financing efforts, on-set coordination, editorial processes, promotional strategy, and release logistics.
checklist_width: Development: securing rights, writing screenplay drafts, creating synopsis/storyboards, producer-led concept shaping
Financing: networking with investors, pitching project materials, meetings and festival markets to secure funds
Pre-production: casting, hiring crew, selecting locations, building sets/props, shot lists and production scheduling
Production: director leading creative decisions, assistant director coordinating schedule, crew (camera, lighting, sound, grips) filming scenes
Post-production: editor assembling footage, collaboration with director, visual effects (if applicable), music composition, sound design, color correction, picture lock
Marketing: teasers, posters, festival screenings, social media campaigns, generating audience buzz
Distribution: domestic and international theatrical agreements, streaming platform deals, physical media (historically), release promotions such as premieres and press
checklist_depth: Identification of the seven stages in correct logical order: Development → Financing → Pre-production → Production → Post-production → Marketing → Distribution
Clear demonstration that each stage is uniquely defined by its core function in the progression from idea to public release
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical sequencing (from concept to release) to require identification of the standard seven stages without naming any specific source (Deep reasoning). It then requires aggregation of distinct responsibilities, roles, and outputs for each stage (Wide scope), ensuring the respondent must compile and differentiate information across all stages rather than relying on a single isolated fact.
source_nodes: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Producing How To's Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production December 12, 2023 The stages of film production involve a meticulous progression, starting with pre-productio
rounds.answer_a:
The seven stages of film production begin with **Development**, where a producer and screenwriter transform an initial concept into a viable script. The primary objective is to secure intellectual property rights and initial financing, with key deliverables being the screenplay, a pitch deck, and a preliminary budget [1, 2].

Next, **Pre-production** involves meticulous planning. The director, producers, and department heads finalize the script, cast actors, scout locations, and create detailed shooting schedules and storyboards. The main goal is to prepare every element needed for filming to ensure a smooth process [3].

The **Production** stage, or principal photography, is the actual filming. Led by the director and cinematographer, the cast and crew work on set to capture all the visual and audio content. The key deliverable from this phase is the raw footage, known as "dailies" [4].

**Post-production** follows, where the film is assembled. Editors cut the footage, while sound designers, composers, and visual effects artists add their respective elements. The objectives are to create a cohesive narrative, complete the sound mix, and produce the final master copy of the film [5].

Simultaneously, **Marketing** aims to build audience anticipation. Marketing executives and publicists create and deploy promotional materials like trailers, posters, and press kits to generate buzz for the upcoming release [3, 6].

**Distribution** is the logistical process of getting the film to audiences. Distributors secure release deals with theaters or streaming platforms and manage the delivery of film prints or digital files [1, 6].

Finally, **Exhibition** is the public release itself, where theaters screen the film or platforms make it available for streaming. This stage's objective is to reach the intended audience and generate revenue [2, 4].

## References
[1] MasterClass. (2022). A Guide to the 7 Stages of Film Production. *MasterClass*.
[2] New York Film Academy. (2022). The 7 Stages of Film Production You Need to Know. *New York Film Academy Blog*.
[3] StudioBinder. (2023). The 5 Stages of Film Production: A Modern Guide for Filmmakers. *StudioBinder*.
[4] No Film School. (2023). The Definitively Unofficial 7 Stages of Film Production. *No Film School*.
[5] Indeed Editorial Team. (2023). The 7 Main Stages of Film Production (With Roles and Duties). *Indeed*.
[6] Backstage. (2022). A Producer's Guide to the 5 Stages of Film Production. *Backstage Magazine*.
rounds.answer_b:
The seven stages of professional film production are as follows:

**1. Development:** The concept is born—scripts are written, stories optioned, and intellectual property secured. Writers, producers, and development executives craft treatments and screenplays. The key deliverable is a polished, greenlit script [1][2].

**2. Pre-Production:** Planning intensifies before cameras roll. The director, producer, line producer, casting director, and department heads assemble. Budgets are finalized, locations scouted, cast hired, storyboards created, and shooting schedules locked [1][3].

**3. Financing:** Capital is secured through studios, investors, tax incentives, pre-sales, or grants. Producers and executive producers lead negotiations. Deliverables include signed financing agreements and a fully funded budget [2][4].

**4. Production:** Principal photography occurs. The director leads on-set coordination alongside the cinematographer, assistant directors, actors, and crew. Daily footage (dailies) is the primary output, captured according to the shooting schedule [1][3].

**5. Post-Production:** Editors, colorists, sound designers, VFX artists, and composers transform raw footage into a finished film. Activities include editing, color grading, sound mixing, scoring, and visual effects. The deliverable is the final cut [1][2].

**6. Marketing & Distribution:** Promotional strategy takes center stage. Marketing teams, distributors, and publicists create trailers, posters, press campaigns, and festival submissions. Distribution deals for theatrical, streaming, and home media are finalized [2][3].

**7. Exhibition/Release:** The film reaches audiences through theaters, streaming platforms, or physical media. Release logistics—scheduling, prints, digital delivery—are managed by distributors and exhibitors, completing the production lifecycle [1][4].

## References
1. StudioBinder. "The 7 Stages of Film Production." https://www.studiobinder.com/blog/film-production-process/
2. MasterClass. "Film Production Guide: The 7 Stages of Filmmaking." https://www.masterclass.com/articles/film-production-guide
3. Nashville Film Institute. "Stages of Film Production." https://www.nfi.edu/stages-of-film-production/
4. Wrapbook. "The Stages of Film Production." https://www.wrapbook.com/blog/stages-of-production
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Both agents fail the required logical order (Development → Financing → Pre-production → Production → Post-production → Marketing → Distribution). Agent A omits Financing as a distinct stage and substitutes Exhibition, which is not part of the required seven-stage progression—this is a major structural error. Agent B includes Financing but places it after Pre-production (incorrect order) and combines Marketing & Distribution into one stage, then adds Exhibition, also deviating from the specified sequence.

Width/Completeness: Agent B more fully covers financing mechanisms (investors, studios, tax incentives, agreements) and clearly distinguishes promotional strategy from release logistics, even if combined structurally. Agent A significantly underrepresents Financing (folded into Development) and never properly addresses investor networking or deal-making as a standalone phase.

Presentation & UX: Both are clearly formatted and readable. Agent A has slightly stronger separation of Marketing and Distribution, but this is outweighed by the structural omission of Financing. Agent B provides better content density and clearer differentiation of responsibilities.

Conclusion: Both have structural errors, but Agent A fails more severely (missing a core stage and incorrect progression). Therefore, Agent B is better overall.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Screenwriting School Screenwriting School Hands-On Degrees, Programs, and Workshops In film, television, and new media, the Screenwriter establishes the basic building bl
rounds.answer_a:
The New York Film Academy (NYFA) outlines the stages of film production and offers specialized training for screenwriters and producers [1]. Its Screenwriting division provides a range of programs that support the development and pre-production stages. Offerings include long-term degree programs like the Master of Fine Arts (MFA) and Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA), alongside intensive one- and two-year conservatory programs that award a certificate upon completion [2, 3]. For more flexible training, NYFA provides short-term workshops ranging from one to twelve weeks, available both in-person and online. These programs concentrate on creating viable screenplays, series bibles, and pitch-ready materials, which are the essential deliverables required to secure financing and move a project forward [3, 4].

For aspiring producers, NYFA offers a fully online Master of Arts (MA) in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation. This one-year, three-semester program is structured for working professionals and integrates creative producing with modern business strategy [5]. The curriculum covers the entire production pipeline, with courses on entertainment law, new media distribution models, project financing, and digital marketing. This structure prepares producers to oversee a project from initial financing through its marketing and distribution phases. The program culminates in a comprehensive business plan for an original entertainment venture, such as a feature film or television series. This final project requires students to develop a complete proposal with financial projections and a detailed distribution strategy, equipping them to launch projects in the contemporary market [5, 6].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *The 7 Stages of Film Production You Need to Know*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[2] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *School of Screenwriting*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[3] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *1-Year Screenwriting Certificate Program*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[4] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online Screenwriting Workshops*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[5] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[6] New York Film Academy. (2023). *2023-2024 Los Angeles Academic Catalog*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is the **American Film Institute (AFI)**, which outlines the seven stages of film production and offers specialized training for screenwriters and producers through its AFI Conservatory and other programs [1][2].

## Screenwriting Division

AFI's screenwriting programs span multiple formats and credential levels. The **AFI Conservatory** offers a **Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Screenwriting**, an intensive two-year, in-person program at its Los Angeles campus where fellows develop original feature and television scripts through workshops, mentorship, and collaborative projects with other disciplines [1][3]. Beyond the MFA, AFI provides shorter-format training through its **AFI Workshops**, including intensive screenwriting workshops designed for working professionals and emerging writers. These are typically shorter-duration, in-person experiences focused on craft development [3]. AFI has also expanded into **online education**, offering digital screenwriting courses and workshops accessible to a broader audience [2][4]. Screenwriting training directly supports the **development and pre-production stages** of film production, where scripts are conceived, drafted, and polished before production begins.

## Online Master's in Producing

AFI offers a **fully online Master of Fine Arts in Producing** focused on **entrepreneurial producing and innovation**. This program is designed for working professionals and can be completed in approximately **two years** [4][5]. The curriculum integrates business strategy, content development, financing, marketing, distribution, and emerging technologies. Students study the full lifecycle of a project—from concept through audience delivery—emphasizing entrepreneurial approaches to the evolving entertainment landscape [4][5]. Coursework covers areas including intellectual property development, packaging, physical and digital distribution models, and leadership. The program culminates in a **capstone project** where candidates develop and present a comprehensive producing portfolio or business plan demonstrating mastery of the production pipeline [5].

## Distinguishing the Two

The screenwriting programs cultivate the **creative storytelling craft** essential to development, while the producing MFA builds **strategic, business, and leadership competencies** spanning financing, production management, marketing, and distribution—together covering virtually all seven stages of filmmaking [1][4].

## References
1. AFI Conservatory. "AFI Conservatory Programs." https://www.afi.com/conservatory/
2. American Film Institute. "AFI Education Overview." https://www.afi.com/education/
3. AFI. "Screenwriting Discipline." https://www.afi.com/conservatory/screenwriting/
4. AFI. "AFI Online MFA in Producing." https://www.afi.com/online-mfa/
5. Variety. "AFI Launches Online MFA in Producing." https://variety.com/2022/film/news/afi-online-mfa-producing-1235184561/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which matches the ground truth. Agent B incorrectly identifies the American Film Institute (AFI), failing the core entity requirement. This is a fundamental DEEP logic error.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most major checklist elements—MFA and BFA degrees, conservatory-style certificate programs, in-person and online workshops, and the fully online three-semester MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation with curriculum elements such as entertainment law, financing, distribution, and marketing. While Agent A omits some specific credentials (e.g., AFA, BFA in Entertainment Media concentration) and does not explicitly mention the e-portfolio capstone or detailed practical deliverables (short films, pitch decks, etc.), it does not introduce factual inaccuracies. The structure and distinctions between screenwriting and producing pathways are clearly explained.

Agent B, despite strong formatting and clarity, builds its entire response around the wrong institution and even describes a different MFA structure and duration, making the answer fundamentally incorrect regardless of presentation quality.

Therefore, because Agent A identifies the correct institution and largely satisfies the required program details—while Agent B fails the core entity check—Agent A is MUCH BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

logic_chain: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
rounds.question: In the context of the film production pipeline, the development stage centers on transforming ideas into structured scripts before a project moves into financing and pre-production. Identify all of the fully online, multi-week screenwriting workshops offered by a major U.S.-based film academy that are specifically designed to support this development phase. For each workshop, specify: (1) its full title and duration, (2) its primary creative focus (e.g., feature film, television spec, pilot, comic book, rewrite, or foundational story structure), (3) any stated prerequisites or submission requirements, and (4) the general tuition range and technical/software expectations common across these online workshops. Your response must comprehensively cover every qualifying online workshop and compare their purposes and entry requirements.
checklist_width: 15-Week Online Screenplay Story & Structure Workshop – focus on feature film development, treatment, beat sheet, no prerequisite stated
15-Week Online Screenplay Workshop – focus on completing a feature screenplay, prerequisite of Story & Structure workshop or submission of treatment and beat sheet
15-Week Online Writing for TV Spec Workshop – focus on writing a spec episode for an existing show, structure and story engine study
15-Week Online Television Pilot Workshop – focus on writing an original TV pilot, prerequisite of TV Spec workshop or submission of formatted TV script
15-Week Online Screenplay & Television Rewrite Workshop – focus on revising/reworking an existing screenplay or pilot
15-Week Online Writing for Comic Books Workshop – focus on comic book/graphic novel scripting and proposal development
4-Week Online Screenwriting – Story And Structure Workshop – focus on foundational story elements and treatment creation
General tuition range of approximately $2,000–$2,500 USD per workshop
Technical requirements: strong internet connection, quiet workspace, use of preferred screenwriting software with discounted Final Draft option

It then demands aggregation of detailed information—titles, durations, focuses, prerequisites, tuition, and technical requirements—across all relevant online workshops (wide scope), ensuring multiple pages or sections must be consulted and synthesized.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: NYFA Screenwriting School Online Screenwriting Workshops Online Screenwriting Workshops Learn Screenwriting Online at NYFA In addition to our in-person programs and workshops,
rounds.answer_a:
The New York Film Academy offers several fully online, multi-week screenwriting workshops designed to support the development stage of production. These programs help writers transform concepts into structured scripts [1].

For foundational learning, the **4-Week Online Screenwriting I** workshop focuses on story structure, character development, and scene construction, culminating in a detailed outline and the first ten pages of a script. It requires no prior experience [2].

Project-specific workshops for beginners include the **12-Week Online Feature Screenwriting**, where students write a complete feature-length screenplay, and the **8-Week Online TV Pilot I**, centered on creating an original television pilot from concept to first draft [1, 3]. The **8-Week Online TV Spec** workshop teaches students to write a sample script for an existing television series, while the **8-Week Online Comic Book Writing** workshop guides them in scripting a 22-page comic and developing a series bible [4, 5]. All these are open to writers without a completed project.

In contrast, advanced workshops have specific submission requirements. The **4-Week Online Rewrite** is designed for writers who already have a complete screenplay and want to focus on revision [6]. Similarly, the **8-Week Online TV Pilot II** requires a finished pilot draft as a prerequisite, focusing on advancing that script to a professional, polished level [3].

Across these workshops, tuition generally ranges from approximately $1,400 to $3,900, varying by duration. Common technical requirements include a modern computer with a webcam, reliable high-speed internet, and screenwriting software like Final Draft, which is often provided or available at a student discount [7].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *12-Week Online Feature Screenwriting Workshop*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[2] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *4-Week Online Screenwriting I Workshop*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[3] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online TV Writing Workshops*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[4] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *8-Week Online TV Spec Workshop*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[5] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *8-Week Online Comic Book Writing Workshop*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[6] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *4-Week Online Rewrite Workshop*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
[7] New York Film Academy. (n.d.). *Online Programs Technology Requirements*. Retrieved from nyfa.edu.
rounds.answer_b:
The **American Film Institute (AFI)** offers a suite of fully online, multi-week screenwriting workshops designed to support the development stage of the film production pipeline. Each workshop targets a distinct creative focus within scriptwriting [1][2].

**1. Screenwriting Fundamentals Workshop** — 6 weeks. Focuses on foundational story structure, character development, and narrative principles essential for any format. No prerequisites or writing samples required, making it the entry point for emerging writers [1][2].

**2. Feature Film Screenwriting Workshop** — 8 weeks. Centers on developing an original feature-length screenplay from concept through a structured outline and initial pages. Participants typically need a completed logline or story concept; some sessions require a prior workshop or writing sample submission [1][2].

**3. Television Spec Screenwriting Workshop** — 8 weeks. Dedicated to writing a spec script for an existing television series, teaching writers to capture an established show's voice and structure. Applicants generally must submit a writing sample or demonstrate familiarity with television writing conventions [1][3].

**4. Television Pilot Screenwriting Workshop** — 8 weeks. Focuses on creating an original TV pilot, including world-building, character bible development, and series-arc planning. A writing sample or completion of a prior AFI workshop is typically expected [1][3].

**5. Rewrite Workshop** — 6 weeks. Designed for writers with a completed draft who need structured guidance on revision, feedback integration, and polishing. Prerequisite: a full-length screenplay or teleplay draft ready for rewrite [1][2].

**6. Comic Book and Graphic Novel Writing Workshop** — 6 weeks. Explores visual storytelling specific to comic book and graphic novel formats, including panel structure and sequential narrative. No strict prerequisites, though a story concept is recommended [1][2].

**Tuition and Technical Requirements:** Tuition across AFI's online workshops generally falls in the range of approximately **$800–$1,500** per workshop, varying by duration and content [1][2]. Technical expectations include a reliable internet connection, a computer capable of video conferencing, and access to industry-standard screenwriting software such as **Final Draft** or equivalent programs [1][3].

**Comparison:** The Fundamentals workshop serves as a gateway requiring no prior experience, while Feature Film, TV Spec, TV Pilot, and Comic Book workshops each target specific formats with progressively focused entry expectations. The Rewrite workshop uniquely requires a completed draft, positioning it later in a writer's development cycle [1][2].

## References
1. AFI. "AFI Online Screenwriting Workshops." https://www.afi.com/education/online-workshops/
2. American Film Institute. "AFI Workshop Overview." https://www.afi.com/education/
3. AFI. "Television Writing Programs." https://www.afi.com/conservatory/screenwriting/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which matches the Ground Truth. Agent B incorrectly identifies the American Film Institute (AFI), which is not the target entity. Therefore, Agent B fails the DEEP logic check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A does not accurately list the required 15-week NYFA Online Screenwriting Workshops (e.g., 15-Week Screenplay Story & Structure, 15-Week Screenplay Workshop, 15-Week TV Spec, 15-Week Television Pilot, 15-Week Rewrite, 15-Week Comic Book, and the 4-Week Story & Structure). Instead, it provides incorrect durations (8-week, 12-week) and omits key workshops, so it fails the WIDE checklist. Tuition range is also inaccurate. Agent B, while structured and clear, is entirely about the wrong institution, so it also fails WIDE relative to the checklist.

Since Agent B fails the core entity (DEEP failure) and Agent A at least identifies NYFA and generally aligns with the development-stage framing, Agent A is the "Flawed Winner." However, because Agent A contains substantial factual inaccuracies in workshop titles, durations, and tuition, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER.

Thus, Agent A provides the better user experience overall due to correct institutional focus and somewhat relevant structure, but both responses are materially flawed.
